questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama membedakan epigenetik dari genetika tradisional dengan menyatakan bahwa epigenetik mempelajari perubahan 'ekspresi gen' tanpa mengubah 'urutan DNA yang mendasarinya'.

questions.questionText: The word 'reductionist' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Oversimplified
(B) Comprehensive
(C) Revolutionary
(D) Optimistic
questions.explanation: 'Reductionist' merujuk pada pandangan yang mencoba menjelaskan fenomena kompleks dengan memecahnya menjadi bagian-bagian yang terlalu sederhana ('oversimplified').

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what is the typical effect of DNA methylation?
options: (A) It unravels the DNA from the histones.
(B) It permanently deletes a gene from the chromosome.
(C) It represses or silences the transcription of a gene.
(D) It speeds up the mutation rate of cytosine bases.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa metilasi DNA biasanya bertindak untuk menekan atau 'membungkam' (silence) transkripsi gen.

questions.questionText: What role do histones play in epigenetic modification?
options: (A) They are chemical tags that destroy toxic DNA.
(B) They are protein spools that, when modified, can loosen DNA and activate genes.
(C) They are enzymes responsible for repairing broken DNA sequences.
(D) They act as a shield to prevent environmental factors from reaching the cell.
questions.explanation: Teks menjelaskan histones sebagai gulungan protein ('protein spools'). Modifikasi pada histones dapat menyebabkan DNA sedikit mengendur ('uncoil slightly'), sehingga 'mengaktifkan' gen.

questions.questionText: The word 'seminal' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Highly influential or original
(B) Flawed and unscientific
(C) Recent and unproven
(D) Strictly theoretical
questions.explanation: 'Seminal' berarti sangat berpengaruh, penting, dan menjadi dasar bagi perkembangan selanjutnya ('highly influential').

questions.questionText: Why did the children of the Dutch Hunger Winter exhibit higher rates of obesity and diabetes later in life?
options: (A) Their underlying DNA sequence was randomly mutated by wartime radiation.
(B) They adopted the poor dietary habits of their parents after the war.
(C) Their epigenomes were reprogrammed in the womb to hoard calories due to famine conditions.
(D) They lacked the histone proteins necessary for proper digestion.
questions.explanation: Paragraf terakhir menjelaskan bahwa trauma kelaparan memprogram ulang epigenom janin untuk 'menimbun kalori' ('hoard calories'), sebuah adaptasi dari masa kelaparan yang menjadi bumerang di masa berlimpah makanan.

questions.questionText: What does the passage imply about the relationship between environment and biology?
options: (A) The environment only affects behavior, not cellular biology.
(B) Biological inheritance can be directly shaped by the environmental experiences of ancestors.
(C) Environmental factors can only influence individuals, not their offspring.
(D) Biological traits are immune to environmental stress once a person reaches adulthood.
questions.explanation: Teks menyiratkan (bahkan menyatakan dengan jelas) bahwa kita mewarisi sifat biologis berdasarkan pengalaman lingkungan leluhur kita (transgenerational epigenetic inheritance).

questions.questionText: The phrase 'maladaptive' in paragraph 4 suggests that the epigenetic changes:
options: (A) Were helpful during the famine but harmful in a food-rich environment.
(B) Completely failed to help the fetuses survive the famine.
(C) Caused immediate genetic diseases at birth.
(D) Were easily reversed once the famine ended.
questions.explanation: 'Maladaptive' berarti tidak sesuai atau merugikan dalam kondisi tertentu. Adaptasi menimbun kalori berguna saat kelaparan, tetapi menjadi merugikan ('maladaptive') di lingkungan pasca-perang yang banyak makanan.

questions.questionText: Which of the following would be an example of an epigenetic change as described in the text?
options: (A) Exposure to UV radiation causing a permanent mutation in a skin cell's DNA sequence.
(B) A person inheriting blue eyes because both parents have the recessive gene for blue eyes.
(C) Chronic stress causing the addition of methyl groups to DNA, shutting down a gene that regulates anxiety.
(D) A virus inserting its own genetic code into a host cell's genome.
questions.explanation: Pilihan C adalah contoh epigenetik yang tepat: faktor lingkungan (stres kronis) menyebabkan penambahan grup metil yang mematikan gen, TANPA mengubah urutan DNA dasar.

passages.title: The Decipherment of Linear B
passages.content:
In the early 20th century, British archaeologist Arthur Evans excavated the ancient ruins of Knossos on the island of Crete, uncovering the remnants of the enigmatic Minoan civilization. Among his most tantalizing finds were thousands of clay tablets inscribed with a mysterious, unknown writing system which he dubbed 'Linear B', distinguishing it from an older, pictorial script he called Linear A. For fifty years, the script of Linear B remained entirely undeciphered, defying the efforts of the world's greatest linguists and cryptographers. Because the Minoan civilization predated the known emergence of Greek culture, the academic consensus firmly held that Linear B represented a distinct, indigenous Minoan language entirely unrelated to Greek.

The breakthrough in decipherment was not the work of a single sudden revelation, but rather a slow, collaborative accumulation of cryptographic analysis. The foundation was laid by Alice Kober, an American classicist who meticulously cataloged the symbols on index cards. Kober identified that certain symbols shared linguistic roots but featured different endings, proving that Linear B was a highly inflected language with grammatical cases and genders. She constructed a theoretical 'grid' that mapped the phonetic relationships between different syllables, even though she did not yet know what those syllables actually sounded like.

Tragically, Kober died before the puzzle was solved, but her methodology was inherited by Michael Ventris, an English architect and brilliant amateur cryptographer. Ventris expanded upon Kober's grid. Operating on a controversial hunch, he hypothesized that some of the most frequently repeating 'words' on the Knossos tablets might be place names—toponyms like Knossos, Amnisos, and Tylissos. When he plugged the phonetic values of these known locations into the grid, a domino effect occurred. The remaining syllables began to form recognizable words.

In 1952, Ventris made a monumental announcement: Linear B was not an unknown Minoan language, but rather an archaic, syllabic form of ancient Greek, predating Homer by roughly five centuries. This revelation sent shockwaves through the fields of archaeology and classical history. It proved that the Mycenaean Greeks from the mainland had conquered Crete and absorbed Minoan bureaucratic administration. Ventris's decipherment radically rewrote the timeline of European history, pushing the documented existence of the Greek language back to the second millennium BCE.

questions.questionText: What is the primary focus of the passage?
options: (A) A detailed comparison of the Minoan and Mycenaean military strategies.
(B) The biography of Arthur Evans and his excavations at Knossos.
(C) The historical process and significance of deciphering the Linear B script.
(D) The grammatical differences between Linear A and Linear B.
questions.explanation: Fokus utama teks adalah menceritakan sejarah bagaimana skrip Linear B dipecahkan (melalui Kober dan Ventris) dan dampak historis dari penemuan tersebut.

questions.questionText: Why did early academics believe Linear B was unrelated to the Greek language?
options: (A) Because the symbols looked exactly like ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics.
(B) Because the Minoan civilization existed before the known emergence of Greek culture.
(C) Because Arthur Evans intentionally hid evidence connecting it to Greece.
(D) Because the tablets were found exclusively on the Greek mainland, not on Crete.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan konsensus akademis percaya Linear B tidak terkait dengan bahasa Yunani karena peradaban Minoan mendahului ('predated') kemunculan budaya Yunani yang diketahui.

questions.questionText: The word 'tantalizing' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Terrifying
(B) Intriguing and tempting
(C) Completely worthless
(D) Easily understood
questions.explanation: 'Tantalizing' berarti sesuatu yang sangat menggoda, menarik, atau memancing rasa ingin tahu, namun sulit dijangkau atau dipahami ('intriguing and tempting').

questions.questionText: What was Alice Kober's crucial contribution to the decipherment of Linear B?
options: (A) She discovered the ruins of Knossos on Crete.
(B) She correctly guessed the phonetic sounds of the entire alphabet.
(C) She proved it was an inflected language and created a grid mapping phonetic relationships.
(D) She realized the script was an archaic form of the Homeric Greek language.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua menjelaskan bahwa Kober membuktikan Linear B adalah bahasa yang berinfleksi dan membangun 'kisi' (grid) teoretis yang memetakan hubungan fonetik antar suku kata.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about Alice Kober's grid?
options: (A) It showed how symbols related to each other structurally before their actual sounds were known.
(B) It was heavily criticized and ignored by Michael Ventris.
(C) It successfully translated the first paragraph of a Minoan poem.
(D) It proved that Linear B was primarily used for writing fictional literature.
questions.explanation: Teks menyatakan bahwa Kober memetakan hubungan fonetik 'meskipun dia belum tahu seperti apa bunyi suku kata itu sebenarnya'. Ini berarti kisi tersebut menunjukkan struktur sebelum suara aslinya diketahui.

questions.questionText: The word 'hunch' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Scientific law
(B) Mathematical proof
(C) Intuitive guess
(D) Physical artifact
questions.explanation: 'Hunch' berarti firasat atau tebakan yang didasarkan pada intuisi ('intuitive guess'), merujuk pada tebakan Ventris tentang nama tempat.

questions.questionText: How did Michael Ventris trigger the 'domino effect' in the decipherment process?
options: (A) By applying the phonetic sounds of known local place names to Kober's grid.
(B) By discovering a bilingual tablet similar to the Rosetta Stone.
(C) By writing a computer program to analyze symbol frequencies.
(D) By translating the script of Linear A first.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menjelaskan bahwa efek domino terjadi ketika Ventris memasukkan nilai fonetik dari lokasi/nama tempat ('toponyms') yang diketahui ke dalam kisi.

questions.questionText: What was the shocking conclusion of Ventris's decipherment?
options: (A) Linear B was a completely fabricated script created by Arthur Evans.
(B) Linear B was actually an early, syllabic form of ancient Greek.
(C) The Minoans had successfully invaded and conquered mainland Greece.
(D) The script contained the lost epic poems of Homer.
questions.explanation: Kesimpulan yang mengejutkan adalah bahwa Linear B bukanlah bahasa Minoan yang tidak dikenal, melainkan bentuk kuno dari bahasa Yunani ('archaic, syllabic form of ancient Greek').

questions.questionText: The word 'predating' in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Destroying
(B) Occurring before
(C) Running concurrently with
(D) Copying from
questions.explanation: 'Predating' berarti ada atau terjadi sebelum waktu tertentu ('occurring before'). Linear B mendahului era Homer.

questions.questionText: What historical implication was derived from the realization that Linear B was Greek?
options: (A) It proved that the Minoans were a peaceful society with no military.
(B) It indicated that mainland Mycenaean Greeks had taken over Minoan Crete.
(C) It suggested that the Greek language was invented on the island of Crete.
(D) It confirmed that Arthur Evans had translated the tablets correctly decades earlier.
questions.explanation: Penemuan tersebut membuktikan bahwa orang Yunani Mycenaean dari daratan telah menaklukkan Kreta dan mengambil alih administrasi birokrasi Minoan ('conquered Crete and absorbed Minoan bureaucratic administration').

passages.title: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM)
passages.content:
Approximately 56 million years ago, the Earth underwent a profound and rapid climatic shift known as the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM). During this epochal transition, global surface temperatures spiked by an astonishing 5 to 8 degrees Celsius (9 to 14 degrees Fahrenheit) over a relatively minuscule geological timeframe—estimated to be as brief as a few thousand years. This hyperthermal event provides modern climatologists with one of the most accurate geological analogs for contemporary anthropogenic climate change, offering critical insights into how the biosphere responds to massive injections of greenhouse gases.

The instigating mechanism of the PETM remains a subject of intense scientific scrutiny, though isotopic records derived from marine sediments point unequivocally to a massive release of carbon into the ocean-atmosphere system. The source of this carbon is debated. The leading hypothesis suggests the destabilization and sudden melting of vast underwater reservoirs of methane clathrates (methane ice) buried in the ocean floor, triggered by initial gradual warming. Other theories implicate immense volcanic activity in the North Atlantic Igneous Province, which may have baked carbon-rich sediments, venting colossal volumes of CO2 and methane into the atmosphere.

The environmental consequences of the PETM were catastrophic and far-reaching. As the oceans absorbed the excess carbon, they underwent severe acidification and warming, particularly in the deep sea. This led to a major extinction event among benthic foraminifera—microscopic, shell-dwelling organisms that thrive on the ocean floor. Their disappearance caused a ripple effect throughout the marine food web. Conversely, the warming at the surface facilitated a massive global migration of terrestrial life. Tropical forests expanded toward the poles, and early mammalian orders, including the ancestors of modern primates, horses, and deer, rapidly dispersed across newly accessible high-latitude land bridges interconnecting North America, Europe, and Asia.

While the PETM bears striking similarities to modern climate change, particularly regarding ocean acidification and rising temperatures, there is a chilling distinction. The rate of carbon emission during the PETM, while exceptionally fast by geological standards, was likely an order of magnitude slower than the current rate of human-induced carbon emissions. This suggests that modern ecosystems may have significantly less time to adapt to thermal and chemical shocks than their Paleocene counterparts did.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
options: (A) A comparison of methane clathrates and volcanic CO2 emissions.
(B) The evolutionary history of early mammalian orders.
(C) A rapid ancient global warming event and its relevance to modern climate change.
(D) The extinction of microscopic sea creatures due to ocean cooling.
questions.explanation: Teks ini secara khusus membahas PETM (peristiwa pemanasan global purba yang cepat) dan relevansinya sebagai analogi (perbandingan) untuk perubahan iklim buatan manusia saat ini.

questions.questionText: Why do climatologists study the PETM?
options: (A) Because it was the only time in Earth's history when volcanoes erupted.
(B) Because it provides an accurate geological analog for current human-induced climate change.
(C) Because they want to harvest the remaining methane clathrates for energy.
(D) Because it caused the immediate extinction of all land-dwelling mammals.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan bahwa peristiwa ini memberikan ahli iklim modern 'analog geologi paling akurat untuk perubahan iklim antropogenik kontemporer'.

questions.questionText: The word 'unequivocally' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Vaguely
(B) Indisputably
(C) Unintentionally
(D) Temporarily
questions.explanation: 'Unequivocally' berarti secara jelas, tanpa keraguan, atau secara mutlak ('indisputably'). Catatan isotop secara mutlak menunjukkan pelepasan karbon besar-besaran.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what are the two leading hypotheses for the source of the massive carbon release during the PETM?
options: (A) Asteroid impacts and solar flares.
(B) Deforestation by early primates and ocean acidification.
(C) Melting of methane clathrates and immense volcanic activity baking carbon-rich sediments.
(D) The extinction of benthic foraminifera and the expansion of tropical forests.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua menyebutkan dua hipotesis: destabilisasi/pencairan reservoir metana klatrat (es metana) di dasar laut, dan aktivitas vulkanik yang memanggang sedimen kaya karbon.

questions.questionText: What happened to benthic foraminifera during the PETM?
options: (A) They migrated to the polar regions.
(B) They experienced a major extinction event due to ocean acidification and warming.
(C) They evolved to live on terrestrial land bridges.
(D) They multiplied rapidly due to the excess carbon in the water.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menjelaskan bahwa asidifikasi dan pemanasan laut dalam menyebabkan 'peristiwa kepunahan besar di antara foraminifera bentik'.

questions.questionText: The word 'facilitated' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Prevented
(B) Complicated
(C) Enabled or made easier
(D) Destroyed
questions.explanation: 'Facilitated' berarti memfasilitasi, melancarkan, atau membuat sesuatu menjadi mungkin/lebih mudah ('enabled or made easier'). Pemanasan memfasilitasi migrasi hewan darat.

questions.questionText: How did the PETM affect early terrestrial mammals?
options: (A) It caused their complete extinction across North America and Europe.
(B) It forced them to adapt to extreme cold and glaciations.
(C) It restricted them to living solely near the equator.
(D) It allowed them to disperse across high-latitude land bridges.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menyatakan bahwa mamalia awal 'menyebar dengan cepat melintasi jembatan darat lintang tinggi yang baru dapat diakses'.

questions.questionText: What is the 'chilling distinction' between the PETM and modern climate change mentioned in the final paragraph?
options: (A) Modern climate change is causing the oceans to cool rather than warm.
(B) The current rate of human-induced carbon emissions is much faster than the emissions during the PETM.
(C) The PETM was caused by humans, whereas modern climate change is natural.
(D) Modern ecosystems have much more time to adapt than Paleocene ecosystems did.
questions.explanation: Perbedaan yang mengerikan adalah bahwa laju emisi saat ini 'satu tingkat besaran (order of magnitude) lebih cepat daripada emisi karbon buatan manusia saat ini' dibandingkan dengan laju selama PETM.

questions.questionText: It can be inferred from the passage that a slower rate of climate change:
options: (A) Always results in higher global temperatures.
(B) Is more likely to trigger volcanic eruptions.
(C) Gives ecosystems a better chance to adapt to environmental shocks.
(D) Prevents tropical forests from expanding.
questions.explanation: Teks menyimpulkan bahwa karena emisi modern lebih cepat, ekosistem memiliki lebih sedikit waktu untuk beradaptasi. Ini menyiratkan (inference) bahwa perubahan yang lebih lambat memberi ekosistem kesempatan lebih baik untuk beradaptasi.

questions.questionText: The tone of the passage can best be described as:
options: (A) Dismissive and skeptical
(B) Scientific and cautionary
(C) Humorous and anecdotal
(D) Excessively optimistic
questions.explanation: Nada penulis objektif secara ilmiah (menjelaskan proses geologi, kimia, dan biologi) namun juga bersifat memperingatkan ('cautionary') tentang konsekuensi laju pemanasan global saat ini.

passages.title: Neuroplasticity and the Myth of the Static Brain
passages.content:
For the majority of the 20th century, the prevailing dogma in neuroscience dictated that the adult human brain was a static, immutably hardwired organ. Scientists widely believed that neurogenesis—the creation of new neurons—ceased entirely shortly after birth, and that the brain's complex neural networks became permanently fixed by the end of childhood. Consequently, neurological damage sustained in adulthood from trauma, stroke, or disease was viewed as inherently irreversible. However, the advent of advanced neuroimaging technologies in the latter decades of the century facilitated a profound paradigm shift, giving rise to the modern concept of neuroplasticity.

Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's remarkable capacity to structurally and functionally reorganize itself in response to learning, experience, and even injury. This malleability is achieved through two primary mechanisms: synaptic plasticity and cortical remapping. Synaptic plasticity involves the strengthening or weakening of the connections (synapses) between individual neurons. This process is governed by Hebb’s postulate, often summarized as 'neurons that fire together, wire together.' When a behavior or cognitive task is repeated, the synaptic connections facilitating that action are fortified, making the action smoother and more automatic over time. Conversely, inactive pathways are subjected to 'synaptic pruning,' conserving metabolic energy for actively used networks.

Cortical remapping, a more macroscopic manifestation of neuroplasticity, occurs when entirely different areas of the brain assume the functions of damaged regions. A striking clinical application of this phenomenon is observed in stroke rehabilitation, particularly through Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy (CIMT). Following a stroke that damages the motor cortex and paralyzes one limb, patients often succumb to 'learned non-use,' relying entirely on their healthy limb. CIMT involves physically restraining the healthy limb and intensely compelling the patient to use the paralyzed one. 

Through rigorous, repetitive task practice, the brain is essentially forced to reroute the motor commands. The sensory and motor functions of the damaged tissue are systematically remapped onto adjacent, healthy cortical tissue. Neuroimaging of stroke survivors undergoing CIMT vividly demonstrates the expansion of functional motor areas into previously unrelated sectors of the cortex. This extraordinary adaptive capability definitively dispels the myth of the static brain, proving that the brain remains a dynamically remodeling organ throughout the entirety of a human lifespan.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: (A) To argue that stroke rehabilitation is mostly ineffective.
(B) To detail the history of neuroimaging technologies.
(C) To explain the concept of neuroplasticity and how it overturned the dogma of the static brain.
(D) To prove that children learn languages faster than adults due to synaptic pruning.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama teks adalah menjelaskan neuroplastisitas (kemampuan otak untuk berubah) dan bagaimana konsep ini mematahkan dogma lama bahwa otak orang dewasa bersifat statis dan tidak dapat diubah.

questions.questionText: What did scientists prior to the late 20th century believe about brain damage sustained in adulthood?
options: (A) It could be easily cured with medication.
(B) It was inherently irreversible because the adult brain was considered static.
(C) It only affected the brain's synaptic pruning process.
(D) It caused the brain to spontaneously create millions of new neurons.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan bahwa kerusakan saraf pada masa dewasa dipandang 'secara inheren tidak dapat diubah' (inherently irreversible) karena otak diyakini secara permanen tetap (statis).

questions.questionText: The word 'malleability' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Fragility
(B) Flexibility and adaptability
(C) Hardness
(D) Complexity
questions.explanation: 'Malleability' berarti kelenturan, kemampuan untuk dibentuk ulang atau beradaptasi ('flexibility and adaptability').

questions.questionText: According to Hebb's postulate ('neurons that fire together, wire together'), what happens when a cognitive task is repeated?
options: (A) The brain creates new lobes to handle the specific task.
(B) Synaptic connections facilitating the action weaken to conserve energy.
(C) Synaptic connections facilitating the action are fortified, making the action more automatic.
(D) The neurons involved undergo immediate synaptic pruning.
questions.explanation: Teks secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa ketika suatu tugas diulang, koneksi sinaptik yang memfasilitasi tindakan tersebut 'diperkuat' ('fortified'), membuat tindakan itu lebih otomatis.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of 'synaptic pruning'?
options: (A) To conserve metabolic energy by eliminating inactive neural pathways.
(B) To force the brain to rely on the healthy limb after a stroke.
(C) To generate entirely new neurons in the cerebral cortex.
(D) To protect the brain from physical trauma.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua menjelaskan bahwa jalur yang tidak aktif mengalami pemangkasan sinaptik ('synaptic pruning') untuk menghemat energi metabolisme bagi jaringan yang aktif digunakan.

questions.questionText: How does Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy (CIMT) work?
options: (A) By electrically stimulating the damaged areas of the motor cortex.
(B) By physically restraining the healthy limb to force the use of the paralyzed limb.
(C) By teaching the patient to rely entirely on their healthy limb for daily tasks.
(D) By administering advanced neuro-regenerative drugs.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menjelaskan bahwa CIMT melibatkan pengekangan fisik ('physically restraining') anggota tubuh yang sehat dan memaksa pasien untuk menggunakan yang lumpuh.

questions.questionText: The phrase 'learned non-use' in paragraph 3 refers to:
options: (A) The brain's inability to learn new concepts after a stroke.
(B) A conscious decision by the patient to ignore the doctor's advice.
(C) A patient relying only on their healthy limb and abandoning the paralyzed one.
(D) The failure of neuroimaging technology to detect brain damage.
questions.explanation: Frasa 'learned non-use' dijelaskan di teks sebagai kondisi di mana pasien bersandar sepenuhnya ('relying entirely') pada anggota badan mereka yang sehat setelah stroke.

questions.questionText: What does neuroimaging demonstrate in stroke survivors who successfully undergo CIMT?
options: (A) The exact regeneration of the originally damaged neurons.
(B) The expansion of functional motor areas into previously unrelated, healthy sectors of the cortex.
(C) A permanent decrease in overall brain activity.
(D) Complete synaptic pruning of the motor cortex.
questions.explanation: Paragraf terakhir menyatakan pencitraan saraf secara jelas menunjukkan ekspansi area motorik fungsional ke sektor korteks sehat yang sebelumnya tidak terkait.

questions.questionText: The word 'dispels' in the final paragraph is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Confirms
(B) Banishes or eliminates
(C) Complicates
(D) Celebrates
questions.explanation: 'Dispels' berarti menghilangkan, membuang, atau mematahkan ('banishes or eliminates') keraguan, mitos, atau kepercayaan palsu (dalam hal ini, mitos otak statis).

questions.questionText: Which of the following best summarizes the paradigm shift discussed in the passage?
options: (A) From viewing the brain as dynamic to viewing it as strictly mechanical.
(B) From believing neurons can regenerate to proving they cannot.
(C) From viewing the adult brain as fixed and unchangeable to understanding it is constantly adaptable.
(D) From treating strokes with physical therapy to treating them exclusively with surgery.
questions.explanation: Pergeseran paradigma yang dibahas adalah transisi dari memandang otak orang dewasa sebagai benda yang statis dan tidak dapat diubah (fixed) menjadi memahaminya sebagai organ yang dapat beradaptasi (neuroplasticity).

passages.title: The Late Bronze Age Collapse: A Systems Failure
passages.content:
During the 12th century BCE, the eastern Mediterranean region experienced a cataclysmic societal unraveling known as the Late Bronze Age Collapse. Within a remarkably brief period of merely half a century, a flourishing, highly interconnected network of formidable empires—including the Mycenaeans in Greece, the Hittites in Anatolia, and the New Kingdom of Egypt—suffered unprecedented devastation. Palatial economies disintegrated, central administrations vanished, and vital trade routes, particularly those essential for the procurement of tin and copper to forge bronze, were abruptly severed. For decades, historians sought a single, monolithic culprit to explain this widespread ruin, frequently attributing the collapse entirely to the violent incursions of the enigmatic 'Sea Peoples,' a loose confederation of maritime marauders.

However, contemporary archaeological and historical consensus has largely abandoned the single-cause theory in favor of a more complex 'systems collapse' model. Scholars now argue that the Late Bronze Age world had become overly specialized and inextricably interdependent. The intricate palatial economies were highly centralized, relying heavily on a continuous flow of luxury goods and raw materials. Consequently, when a series of exogenous shocks occurred simultaneously, the entire network proved alarmingly fragile. 

Recent paleoclimatological data has provided compelling evidence that a severe, multi-decadal drought plagued the eastern Mediterranean precisely during this period. This prolonged desiccation precipitated widespread agricultural failure, leading to famine, internal rebellions, and mass migrations. The 'Sea Peoples' are now widely interpreted not merely as bloodthirsty invaders, but largely as desperate climate refugees displaced by crop failures in their homelands, seeking sustenance and habitable territory. Furthermore, archaeoseismology indicates that a 'paroxysm' of destructive earthquakes—a phenomenon known as an earthquake storm—struck Greece and the eastern Mediterranean, physically leveling major urban centers.

The confluence of these factors—protracted drought, systemic famine, seismic activity, and subsequent mass migrations and warfare—created a devastating multiplier effect. The highly centralized bureaucracies, already strained by ecological disasters, could not withstand the sudden disruption of international trade and the influx of hostile, displaced populations. The resulting collapse ushered in a 'Dark Age' characterized by the loss of literacy, monumental architecture, and sophisticated art, a stark reminder of the vulnerability inherent in complex, hyper-connected civilizations.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: (A) To argue that the Sea Peoples were solely responsible for the destruction of the Mycenaean empire.
(B) To detail the specific trading routes used to transport tin and copper in the ancient world.
(C) To explain the transition from a single-cause theory to a multi-causal 'systems collapse' model regarding the fall of Bronze Age civilizations.
(D) To describe the architectural achievements that were lost during the Mediterranean Dark Age.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama teks ini adalah menjelaskan bagaimana pandangan sejarawan bergeser dari menyalahkan satu penyebab (Sea Peoples) menjadi menggunakan model keruntuhan sistem (systems collapse) yang multi-kausal akibat berbagai tekanan bersamaan.

questions.questionText: The word 'cataclysmic' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Gradual
(B) Disastrous
(C) Predictable
(D) Unnoticed
questions.explanation: 'Cataclysmic' berarti menyebabkan kehancuran atau bencana besar yang tiba-tiba, sinonim yang paling tepat adalah 'disastrous' (membawa bencana).

questions.questionText: According to the systems collapse model, why were the Late Bronze Age empires particularly vulnerable?
options: (A) They lacked the military technology to fight at sea.
(B) They had exhausted their domestic supplies of copper and tin.
(C) They were overly specialized, highly centralized, and deeply interdependent on international trade.
(D) They had completely abandoned agriculture in favor of producing luxury goods.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa peradaban tersebut rentan karena mereka menjadi 'overly specialized and inextricably interdependent' dan memiliki ekonomi istana yang terpusat yang sangat bergantung pada perdagangan.

questions.questionText: What does the passage imply about the 'Sea Peoples'?
options: (A) They possessed superior bronze weaponry compared to the Egyptians.
(B) They were likely motivated by starvation and environmental displacement rather than pure malice.
(C) They intentionally caused the multi-decadal drought using advanced agricultural techniques.
(D) They successfully rebuilt the palatial economies after conquering the Mediterranean.
questions.explanation: Teks menyiratkan bahwa mereka bukan sekadar penjajah haus darah, melainkan 'pengungsi iklim yang putus asa yang telantar karena kegagalan panen' (desperate climate refugees displaced by crop failures).

questions.questionText: The word 'inextricably' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Inseparably
(B) Temporarily
(C) Reluctantly
(D) Coincidentally
questions.explanation: 'Inextricably' berarti terhubung sedemikian rupa sehingga tidak dapat dilepaskan atau dipisahkan. 'Inseparably' adalah sinonim yang tepat.

questions.questionText: Why does the author mention 'archaeoseismology' in paragraph 3?
options: (A) To prove that early humans could predict earthquakes.
(B) To introduce physical evidence of another stressor (earthquakes) that contributed to the systemic collapse.
(C) To suggest that earthquakes were the single true cause of the Late Bronze Age Collapse.
(D) To explain how the 'Sea Peoples' navigated the Mediterranean.
questions.explanation: Penulis menyebutkan arkeoseismologi untuk memperkenalkan bukti adanya badai gempa bumi, yang merupakan guncangan eksternal (stresor) lain yang berkontribusi pada keruntuhan sistem.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, all of the following contributed to the Late Bronze Age Collapse EXCEPT:
options: (A) Widespread agricultural failure due to drought.
(B) The sudden severing of international trade routes.
(C) The depletion of gold mines in the New Kingdom of Egypt.
(D) A concentrated series of destructive earthquakes.
questions.explanation: Pilihan A (kekeringan), B (putusnya rute perdagangan), dan D (gempa bumi) semuanya disebutkan. Teks tidak pernah menyebutkan tentang penipisan tambang emas di Mesir (C).

questions.questionText: What happened to the region immediately following the systems collapse?
options: (A) It transitioned into a period of unprecedented democratic rule.
(B) It was fully annexed by the expanding Roman Empire.
(C) It experienced a 'Dark Age' marked by the loss of literacy and monumental architecture.
(D) It rapidly industrialized to mass-produce iron weapons.
questions.explanation: Paragraf terakhir secara langsung menyatakan bahwa keruntuhan tersebut mengantarkan pada 'Dark Age' (Zaman Kegelapan) yang ditandai dengan hilangnya literasi dan arsitektur monumental.

questions.questionText: The phrase 'multiplier effect' in paragraph 4 refers to a situation where:
options: (A) The combination of multiple crises causes a significantly more devastating outcome than any single crisis would alone.
(B) The population of an empire grows too fast for its agricultural sector to support.
(C) An army uses advanced weaponry to defeat a much larger enemy force.
(D) Economic wealth is distributed equally among the citizens of an empire.
questions.explanation: 'Multiplier effect' (efek pengganda) dalam konteks ini merujuk pada bagaimana gabungan berbagai faktor (kekeringan, kelaparan, gempa, perang) saling memperburuk dan menciptakan dampak yang jauh lebih menghancurkan.

questions.questionText: What lesson does the author suggest can be drawn from the Late Bronze Age Collapse?
options: (A) Maritime empires are historically more stable than land-based empires.
(B) Complex, hyper-connected societies possess an inherent vulnerability to systemic shocks.
(C) The invention of bronze was ultimately detrimental to human progress.
(D) Climate change only affects civilizations that lack centralized governments.
questions.explanation: Kalimat penutup berbunyi: 'sebuah pengingat yang nyata akan kerentanan yang melekat pada peradaban yang kompleks dan hiper-terhubung' (a stark reminder of the vulnerability inherent in complex, hyper-connected civilizations).

passages.title: Paleomagnetism and the Proof of Seafloor Spreading
passages.content:
In 1912, meteorologist Alfred Wegener proposed the radical theory of continental drift, positing that the Earth’s landmasses were once joined in a supercontinent called Pangea and had since ploughed through the oceanic crust to their current positions. Wegener’s hypothesis was vehemently rejected by the geological establishment of his time, primarily because he could not formulate a plausible biomechanical mechanism to explain the colossal forces required to move continents. It was not until the mid-20th century, following extensive sonar mapping of the ocean floor during World War II, that the missing mechanism was discovered, fundamentally vindicating Wegener’s core idea.

The critical breakthrough emerged from the study of the mid-ocean ridges—massive, continuous underwater mountain ranges that encircle the globe. In 1960, American geologist Harry Hess proposed the theory of seafloor spreading. Hess theorized that the ocean floor was not static but was constantly being created. He suggested that molten magma from the Earth’s mantle rises at the crests of the mid-ocean ridges, cools to form new basaltic oceanic crust, and subsequently spreads laterally away from the ridge, acting like a giant geological conveyor belt. As this new crust moves outward, it inevitably carries the continents along with it, providing the very mechanism Wegener had lacked.

The definitive proof for Hess’s theory came a few years later via the Vine-Matthews-Morley hypothesis, which brilliantly utilized the burgeoning field of paleomagnetism. As the rising magma at the mid-ocean ridges cools and solidifies into basalt, iron-rich minerals within the rock, such as magnetite, align themselves with the Earth's prevailing magnetic field, essentially locking in a permanent magnetic record of that specific geological moment. 

Crucially, the Earth's magnetic field periodically undergoes 'geomagnetic reversals,' where the magnetic north and south poles spontaneously switch polarity. When scientists towed magnetometers across the mid-ocean ridges, they discovered remarkable anomalies: a striking, symmetrical pattern of alternating magnetic stripes running parallel to the ridge. One stripe exhibited normal polarity (matching today's magnetic field), while the adjacent stripe exhibited reversed polarity. This symmetrical zebra-stripe pattern was undeniable evidence that the ocean floor was being extruded continuously over millions of years, recording the Earth's shifting magnetic field as it spread outward, thereby establishing the foundation for the modern theory of plate tectonics.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
options: (A) The biography of Alfred Wegener and his struggles against the geological establishment.
(B) The process of geomagnetic pole reversals and its effect on modern navigation.
(C) The discovery of seafloor spreading and how paleomagnetism proved the movement of tectonic plates.
(D) The sonar mapping techniques developed by the military during World War II.
questions.explanation: Teks ini berfokus pada bagaimana teori penyebaran dasar laut (seafloor spreading) ditemukan dan bagaimana paleomagnetisme (catatan kemagnetan purba pada bebatuan) membuktikan bahwa lempeng tektonik benar-benar bergerak.

questions.questionText: Why was Alfred Wegener's theory of continental drift initially rejected?
options: (A) He believed that the ocean floor was completely flat and featureless.
(B) He failed to provide a scientifically plausible mechanism to explain how massive continents could actually move.
(C) He based his theory solely on paleomagnetism, which was not yet a respected scientific field.
(D) He proposed that the continents were moving toward each other to form Pangea, rather than drifting apart.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan teorinya ditolak keras 'terutama karena ia tidak dapat merumuskan mekanisme biomekanik yang masuk akal' untuk menjelaskan gaya yang dibutuhkan untuk menggerakkan benua.

questions.questionText: The word 'vindicating' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Disproving
(B) Ridiculing
(C) Proving right
(D) Complicating
questions.explanation: 'Vindicating' berarti membenarkan, membersihkan dari tuduhan, atau membuktikan bahwa seseorang itu benar setelah sebelumnya diragukan ('proving right').

questions.questionText: According to Harry Hess's theory, where is new oceanic crust formed?
options: (A) At the coastlines where the ocean meets the continents.
(B) At the crests of the mid-ocean ridges where magma rises and cools.
(C) Deep within the Earth's core before being pushed to the surface.
(D) In the deepest trenches of the ocean floor.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua menyatakan bahwa magma cair naik 'di puncak punggungan tengah laut (crests of the mid-ocean ridges), mendingin untuk membentuk kerak samudera basaltik baru'.

questions.questionText: What role does magnetite play in the study of paleomagnetism?
options: (A) It melts at low temperatures to lubricate the movement of tectonic plates.
(B) It aligns with the Earth's magnetic field as magma cools, recording the magnetic polarity of that era.
(C) It causes the Earth's magnetic poles to reverse every few million years.
(D) It interferes with sonar equipment, making ocean mapping difficult.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menjelaskan bahwa mineral kaya besi seperti magnetit menyelaraskan diri dengan medan magnet bumi saat mendingin, 'mengunci rekor magnetik permanen dari momen geologis tersebut'.

questions.questionText: The word 'extruded' in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Pushed or forced out
(B) Destroyed completely
(C) Hidden underground
(D) Chemically dissolved
questions.explanation: 'Extruded' berarti didorong, ditekan, atau dikeluarkan melalui suatu celah ('pushed or forced out'), merujuk pada magma yang keluar menjadi kerak baru.

questions.questionText: What did the 'symmetrical zebra-stripe pattern' discovered by magnetometers signify?
options: (A) That the ocean floor was composed of two distinctly different types of rock.
(B) That the Earth's magnetic field has remained constant and unchanging since its formation.
(C) That new crust was being continuously created at the ridge and pushing outward equally in both directions.
(D) That the mid-ocean ridges were formed by massive meteorite impacts.
questions.explanation: Pola simetris di kedua sisi punggung bukit menunjukkan bahwa kerak baru terus-menerus tercipta di tengah dan mendorong keluar secara merata ke kedua arah selama jutaan tahun (merekam pembalikan magnetik).

questions.questionText: What can be inferred from the passage about the relationship between Hess's theory and Wegener's theory?
options: (A) Hess's theory completely contradicted Wegener's idea of moving continents.
(B) Hess's theory provided the mechanical explanation that Wegener's theory of continental drift had lacked.
(C) Hess and Wegener collaborated closely to map the mid-ocean ridges.
(D) Hess believed the continents were moving independently of the ocean floor, unlike Wegener.
questions.explanation: Teks secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa kerak samudera yang menyebar bertindak seperti ban berjalan yang membawa benua, 'memberikan mekanisme yang tidak dimiliki Wegener'.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage as a step in the process of seafloor spreading?
options: (A) Magma rises from the Earth's mantle.
(B) Magma cools and forms basaltic crust.
(C) The old crust sinks back into the mantle at deep ocean trenches.
(D) The newly formed crust spreads laterally away from the ridge.
questions.explanation: Pilihan A, B, dan D semuanya dijelaskan di paragraf kedua. Proses subduksi (kerak lama tenggelam kembali ke mantel di palung dalam) tidak disebutkan dalam bagian teks ini.

questions.questionText: Why does the author use the phrase 'geological conveyor belt' in paragraph 2?
options: (A) To highlight the industrial pollution occurring in the oceans.
(B) To describe the speed at which marine animals migrate across the ocean.
(C) To vividly illustrate how the continuous creation and outward movement of the seafloor transports the continents.
(D) To mock the simplistic nature of Harry Hess's initial theory.
questions.explanation: Metafora 'ban berjalan geologis' digunakan untuk memberikan gambaran visual yang jelas tentang bagaimana kerak baru terus dibuat di tengah dan bergerak ke luar, mengangkut apa pun yang ada di atasnya (benua).

passages.title: The Harlem Renaissance: Art as Social Catalyst
passages.content:
Spanning roughly from the end of World War I through the mid-1930s, the Harlem Renaissance stands as one of the most vibrant and consequential intellectual, literary, and artistic movements in American history. The genesis of this cultural explosion was inextricably linked to the Great Migration, a demographic shift wherein hundreds of thousands of African Americans relocated from the oppressive, strictly segregated rural South to the industrialized urban centers of the North, seeking economic opportunity and sanctuary from institutionalized racial violence. Harlem, a neighborhood in Manhattan, quickly coalesced into the demographic and cultural epicenter of this diaspora, attracting a dense concentration of Black artists, scholars, musicians, and writers.

The philosophical architect of the movement was arguably Alain Locke, a Harvard-educated philosopher who codified the era's ethos in his seminal 1925 anthology, 'The New Negro.' Locke articulated a paradigm shift away from the subservient, apologetic caricature of the 'Old Negro' imposed by white supremacy. Instead, he championed the 'New Negro,' characterized by a robust self-respect, cultural pride, and a militant demand for civil and political rights. Locke contended that African Americans could achieve equality and dismantle racial prejudice not merely through political agitation, but by proving their intellectual and artistic parity on the world stage. 

This ideology manifested in a breathtaking outpouring of creative expression. Writers like Langston Hughes and Zora Neale Hurston eschewed European literary conventions, choosing instead to elevate the authentic cadence, vernacular, and folklore of the Black working class. Hughes's poetry, for instance, rhythmically mirrored the syncopated beats of jazz and blues—musical genres pioneered by Black musicians that were simultaneously taking the world by storm. 

However, the movement was not without profound internal dissonance. A central dichotomy fiercely debated among its intelligentsia was whether Black art should conform to traditional Western aesthetic standards to prove equality to a white audience, or whether it should forge a distinct, separate cultural identity rooted in African heritage and the unique African American experience. While the Great Depression ultimately stifled the movement's economic patronage, bringing its formal epoch to a close, the Harlem Renaissance irreversibly altered the trajectory of American culture, laying the indispensable psychological and cultural groundwork for the Civil Rights Movement of the mid-20th century.

questions.questionText: What is the primary focus of the passage?
options: (A) The economic impacts of the Great Depression on the northern United States.
(B) The musical evolution of jazz and blues in the early 20th century.
(C) The origins, philosophy, creative output, and legacy of the Harlem Renaissance.
(D) A biographical comparison of Alain Locke and Langston Hughes.
questions.explanation: Teks ini mencakup gambaran komprehensif tentang Harlem Renaissance: pemicunya (Great Migration), filosofinya (Alain Locke), karyanya (Hughes, Hurston), perdebatan internalnya, dan warisan jangka panjangnya.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what was a primary cause of the Great Migration?
options: (A) The desire of African Americans to return to their ancestral homeland in Africa.
(B) A government mandate forcing agricultural workers to move to urban centers.
(C) The search for economic opportunities and escape from institutionalized racial violence in the South.
(D) The sudden availability of cheap housing in the Manhattan neighborhood of Harlem.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan bahwa ratusan ribu orang Afrika-Amerika pindah dari Selatan ke Utara untuk 'mencari peluang ekonomi dan tempat berlindung dari kekerasan rasial yang dilembagakan'.

questions.questionText: The word 'coalesced' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Disintegrated
(B) Came together to form a single group or mass
(C) Violently rebelled
(D) Remained entirely hidden
questions.explanation: 'Coalesced' berarti bersatu, bergabung, atau mengelompok menjadi satu kesatuan ('came together to form a single group'). Harlem bersatu menjadi pusat budaya.

questions.questionText: How did Alain Locke believe African Americans could best dismantle racial prejudice?
options: (A) By initiating violent political revolutions across the urban North.
(B) By isolating themselves entirely from white society.
(C) By proving their intellectual and artistic equality on the world stage.
(D) By adopting the traditional caricatures of the 'Old Negro'.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua secara eksplisit menyatakan Locke berpendapat bahwa kesetaraan bisa dicapai 'dengan membuktikan paritas intelektual dan artistik mereka di panggung dunia'.

questions.questionText: What does the concept of the 'New Negro' represent according to the passage?
options: (A) An African American who seeks to migrate back to the rural South.
(B) An identity characterized by self-respect, cultural pride, and a demand for civil rights.
(C) A literary character created entirely by white authors of the 1920s.
(D) An artist who exclusively uses European literary conventions.
questions.explanation: Teks menggambarkan 'New Negro' (Negro Baru) yang dikemukakan Locke sebagai sesuatu yang dicirikan oleh 'rasa hormat diri yang kuat, kebanggaan budaya, dan tuntutan militan untuk hak-hak sipil'.

questions.questionText: The word 'eschewed' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Perfected
(B) Memorized
(C) Deliberately avoided
(D) Enthusiastically embraced
questions.explanation: 'Eschew' berarti sengaja menghindari atau menjauhi ('deliberately avoided'). Hughes dan Hurston menghindari konvensi sastra Eropa.

questions.questionText: Why does the author mention the poetry of Langston Hughes?
options: (A) To show an artist who tried to perfectly mimic classic British poetry.
(B) To provide an example of art that elevated Black vernacular and mirrored the rhythms of jazz.
(C) To prove that literature was less important than music during the Harlem Renaissance.
(D) To highlight the lack of creativity among the movement's writers.
questions.explanation: Puisi Hughes disebutkan sebagai contoh penerapan ideologi gerakan tersebut: mengangkat vernakular (bahasa sehari-hari) kelas pekerja dan secara ritmis meniru irama jazz/blues.

questions.questionText: What was the central 'dichotomy' debated among the intelligentsia of the Harlem Renaissance?
options: (A) Whether to support the United States in World War I or remain pacifists.
(B) Whether to stay in Harlem or migrate back to the rural South.
(C) Whether Black art should conform to Western standards to prove equality, or forge a distinctly separate cultural identity.
(D) Whether jazz was a respectable art form or simply popular entertainment.
questions.explanation: Paragraf keempat menjelaskan dikotomi sentral ini: menyesuaikan diri dengan standar estetika Barat (untuk membuktikan kesetaraan) ATAU membentuk identitas budaya yang berbeda berdasarkan warisan Afrika.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what event caused the formal end of the Harlem Renaissance?
options: (A) The start of World War II.
(B) The passage of the Civil Rights Act.
(C) The sudden death of Alain Locke.
(D) The Great Depression, which stifled economic patronage for the arts.
questions.explanation: Teks menyatakan bahwa 'Great Depression pada akhirnya melumpuhkan patronase ekonomi gerakan tersebut, membawa epok formalnya berakhir'.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about the Civil Rights Movement of the mid-20th century from this passage?
options: (A) It was entirely disconnected from the artistic achievements of the 1920s.
(B) It relied heavily on the psychological and cultural foundation established by the Harlem Renaissance.
(C) It was opposed by the remaining figures of the Harlem Renaissance.
(D) It was primarily focused on passing legislation related to the arts.
questions.explanation: Kalimat terakhir menyatakan bahwa Harlem Renaissance meletakkan 'landasan psikologis dan budaya yang sangat diperlukan' untuk Gerakan Hak Sipil, menyiratkan bahwa gerakan tersebut sangat bergantung pada fondasi ini.

passages.title: The Molecular Machinery of Circadian Rhythms
passages.content:
Virtually all living organisms, from simple cyanobacteria to complex mammals, possess an innate biological timing system known as a circadian rhythm. Derived from the Latin 'circa' (around) and 'diem' (day), these endogenous 24-hour cycles govern a vast array of physiological and behavioral processes, including sleep-wake cycles, hormone secretion, cellular regeneration, and core body temperature. In humans, the master pacemaker coordinating these rhythms resides in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), a tiny cluster of approximately 20,000 neurons located in the hypothalamus, situated directly above the optic chiasm.

The groundbreaking research that elucidated the precise molecular mechanism of this internal clock garnered the 2017 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. Using the fruit fly (Drosophila melanogaster) as a model organism, scientists isolated a gene appropriately named 'period' (PER). They discovered that the PER gene encodes a specific protein that accumulates in the cell's cytoplasm during the night and subsequently degrades during the day. 

The core of the circadian clock operates via a sophisticated transcription-translation negative feedback loop. During the day, the PER gene is actively transcribed into messenger RNA, which is translated into the PER protein. As the protein levels peak in the cytoplasm during the night, the PER proteins bind with another protein called TIM (timeless). This PER-TIM complex is then able to penetrate the cell nucleus, where it directly inhibits the very transcription factors responsible for activating the PER gene. By blocking its own production, the protein level eventually drops as it degrades over the course of the day, lifting the inhibition and allowing the cycle to begin anew the following evening.

While this genetic clock is remarkably autonomous—capable of 'free-running' in constant darkness—it is not isolated from the external environment. To prevent the internal clock from drifting out of sync with the Earth's 24-hour rotation, it must undergo a daily resetting process known as entrainment. The primary zeitgeber (time-giver) for entrainment is light. Specialized photosensitive retinal ganglion cells in the eye detect environmental light levels and transmit this information directly to the SCN via the retinohypothalamic tract. This photic input rapidly degrades the TIM protein, prematurely breaking the feedback loop and ensuring that the organism’s internal physiology remains perfectly synchronized with the external solar cycle.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: (A) To explain why humans need an average of eight hours of sleep per night.
(B) To describe the molecular genetics and entrainment process of the biological circadian clock.
(C) To compare the circadian rhythms of fruit flies with those of cyanobacteria.
(D) To argue that modern artificial lighting is destroying human health.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama teks adalah menjelaskan mekanisme molekuler dari ritme sirkadian (bagaimana gen PER dan TIM beroperasi dalam loop umpan balik) dan proses entrainment (penyesuaian dengan cahaya lingkungan).

questions.questionText: The word 'endogenous' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Internally generated
(B) Environmentally harmful
(C) Extremely brief
(D) Consciously controlled
questions.explanation: 'Endogenous' merujuk pada sesuatu yang berasal atau diproduksi dari dalam organisme itu sendiri. 'Internally generated' adalah sinonim yang tepat untuk ritme biologis bawaan.

questions.questionText: Where is the master pacemaker of the human circadian rhythm located?
options: (A) In the optic chiasm of the eye.
(B) In the cell's cytoplasm.
(C) In the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) within the hypothalamus.
(D) In the transcription factors of the DNA.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa alat pacu utama pada manusia 'terletak di suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN)... yang berlokasi di hipotalamus'.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what happens to the PER protein during the night?
options: (A) It completely degrades and disappears from the cell.
(B) It accumulates in the cytoplasm and binds with the TIM protein.
(C) It transforms into messenger RNA.
(D) It exits the cell to signal the suprachiasmatic nucleus.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua dan ketiga menjelaskan bahwa saat malam hari, tingkat protein PER mencapai puncaknya (terakumulasi) di sitoplasma dan berikatan dengan protein TIM.

questions.questionText: How does the negative feedback loop of the circadian clock function?
options: (A) The PER-TIM complex enters the nucleus and blocks the gene from creating more PER protein.
(B) Sunlight directly damages the DNA, stopping the production of all proteins.
(C) The cell runs out of messenger RNA, naturally halting the cycle.
(D) The brain sends a conscious signal to the body to stop producing hormones.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menjelaskan loop umpan balik negatif: kompleks PER-TIM menembus inti sel dan secara langsung menghambat (memblokir) faktor transkripsi yang bertanggung jawab untuk mengaktifkan gen PER (memblokir produksinya sendiri).

questions.questionText: The word 'elucidated' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Confused
(B) Made clear or explained
(C) Accidentally discovered
(D) Heavily criticized
questions.explanation: 'Elucidate' berarti membuat sesuatu menjadi jelas; menerangkan atau menjelaskan ('made clear or explained').

questions.questionText: What does it mean when the genetic clock is described as 'free-running'?
options: (A) It operates faster than a 24-hour cycle.
(B) It continues to function autonomously even without external environmental cues like light.
(C) It completely stops functioning if the organism is kept in constant darkness.
(D) It randomly changes its cycle length from day to day.
questions.explanation: Istilah 'free-running' yang disebutkan dalam teks merujuk pada kemampuan jam tersebut untuk terus beroperasi secara mandiri ('autonomous') bahkan dalam keadaan gelap gulita secara konstan.

questions.questionText: What is a 'zeitgeber' in the context of the passage?
options: (A) A protein that destroys the PER gene.
(B) A specific cluster of neurons in the fruit fly.
(C) An environmental cue, primarily light, that helps reset and synchronize the internal clock.
(D) A sleep disorder caused by a malfunctioning hypothalamus.
questions.explanation: Teks mendefinisikan 'zeitgeber' sebagai 'pemberi waktu' ('time-giver') utama untuk proses penyelarasan (entrainment), yang utamanya adalah cahaya lingkungan yang menyinkronkan jam internal dengan siklus luar.

questions.questionText: How does environmental light affect the molecular clock mechanism?
options: (A) It rapidly degrades the TIM protein, prematurely breaking the feedback loop to reset the clock.
(B) It causes the SCN to produce more PER protein.
(C) It permanently damages the photosensitive retinal ganglion cells.
(D) It forces the PER-TIM complex to remain permanently locked inside the nucleus.
questions.explanation: Paragraf terakhir secara eksplisit menyatakan bahwa input fotik (cahaya) 'secara cepat mendegradasi protein TIM, secara prematur memutus loop umpan balik' untuk menyinkronkan jam.

questions.questionText: Which of the following can be inferred from the passage about people who are entirely blind and lack light perception?
options: (A) They do not possess a suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN).
(B) Their bodies are unable to produce the PER protein.
(C) They may struggle with their circadian rhythms drifting out of sync with the 24-hour solar day.
(D) They have a circadian rhythm that operates on a 12-hour cycle rather than a 24-hour cycle.
questions.explanation: Karena teks menyatakan bahwa cahaya ditangkap oleh mata untuk mengatur ulang jam sirkadian (mencegahnya melenceng/drifting), masuk akal untuk menyimpulkan bahwa individu tanpa persepsi cahaya mungkin mengalami ritme sirkadian yang tidak sinkron.

passages.title: Quantum Computing and the Cryptographic Crisis
passages.content:
For decades, the seemingly inexorable march of computing power was accurately predicted by Moore’s Law, which stated that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles roughly every two years. However, as transistors approach the size of individual atoms, classical computing is colliding with the hard boundaries of physical laws. Enter quantum computing: a revolutionary paradigm that discards the binary logic of classical physics in favor of the bizarre, counterintuitive rules of quantum mechanics. 

Classical computers process information in 'bits', which must exist in a definitive state of either 0 or 1. Quantum computers, conversely, utilize 'qubits' (quantum bits). Thanks to a quantum property known as superposition, a qubit can exist in a state of 0, 1, or critically, any quantum superposition of both states simultaneously. Furthermore, qubits can be 'entangled', a phenomenon where the state of one qubit becomes intrinsically linked to the state of another, regardless of the physical distance separating them. These properties allow a quantum computer to evaluate a vast multitude of computational possibilities concurrently, rather than sequentially, granting them exponential speed advantages for specific types of complex mathematical problems.

While this represents a monumental leap for fields like molecular modeling and material science, it presents an existential threat to modern digital security. Currently, the vast majority of global digital communications—including financial transactions, secure emails, and classified military data—are protected by public-key cryptography algorithms, most notably RSA. The security of RSA relies on an elegant mathematical trapdoor: it is extraordinarily easy for a classical computer to multiply two massive prime numbers together, but it is practically impossible for a classical computer to perform the reverse operation (factoring the resulting massive number back into its original primes) within a human lifetime.

However, in 1994, mathematician Peter Shor developed a quantum algorithm—now known as Shor’s Algorithm—that demonstrated how a sufficiently powerful quantum computer could factor massive integers exponentially faster than any known classical algorithm. A functioning quantum computer running Shor’s Algorithm could shatter RSA encryption in a matter of hours. 

Fortunately for global security, building a practical, large-scale quantum computer remains immensely difficult. Qubits are extraordinarily fragile and highly susceptible to 'decoherence', where minor environmental disturbances like thermal fluctuations or electromagnetic radiation cause them to lose their quantum state and produce calculation errors. Overcoming decoherence via robust quantum error correction remains the most formidable engineering hurdle before the cryptographic crisis becomes a reality.

questions.questionText: What is the primary focus of the passage?
options: (A) To explain why Moore's Law will continue to hold true indefinitely.
(B) To detail the history of RSA encryption and public-key cryptography.
(C) To describe the basic mechanics of quantum computing and its specific threat to modern cryptographic systems.
(D) To propose a new method for manufacturing microscopic transistors.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama teks ini adalah memperkenalkan komputasi kuantum (qubits, superposisi, entanglement) dan kemudian menjelaskan bagaimana ia mengancam enkripsi modern melalui Algoritma Shor, serta kendala teknis saat ini.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, why is classical computing approaching a physical limit?
options: (A) Because classical algorithms have solved all known mathematical problems.
(B) Because there is a global shortage of silicon needed for microchips.
(C) Because transistors are becoming so small they are reaching the scale of individual atoms.
(D) Because quantum computers have already replaced all classical computers.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan bahwa saat 'transistor mendekati ukuran atom individu, komputasi klasik bertabrakan dengan batas keras hukum fisika'.

questions.questionText: How does a 'qubit' fundamentally differ from a classical 'bit'?
options: (A) A qubit can only hold a value of 0, whereas a bit can hold 0 or 1.
(B) A qubit can exist as 0, 1, or both states simultaneously due to superposition.
(C) A qubit relies on thermal fluctuations to process data.
(D) A qubit is a purely theoretical concept that has never been created physically.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua secara eksplisit menjelaskan bahwa berkat superposisi, qubit dapat berada dalam keadaan 0, 1, atau 'keduanya secara bersamaan', tidak seperti bit klasik yang harus berupa 0 atau 1.

questions.questionText: The word 'concurrently' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Randomly
(B) Sequentially
(C) Simultaneously
(D) Erroneously
questions.explanation: 'Concurrently' berarti terjadi pada waktu yang bersamaan. Sinonim yang paling tepat adalah 'simultaneously' (secara bersamaan).

questions.questionText: What mathematical difficulty forms the foundation of modern RSA encryption security?
options: (A) The difficulty of multiplying two large numbers.
(B) The difficulty of factoring a massive number back into its original prime numbers.
(C) The difficulty of converting binary code into quantum entanglement.
(D) The difficulty of predicting environmental thermal fluctuations.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menyatakan bahwa keamanan RSA bergantung pada fakta bahwa sangat sulit (hampir tidak mungkin bagi komputer klasik) untuk memfaktorkan angka besar kembali menjadi bilangan prima aslinya.

questions.questionText: Why is Shor's Algorithm considered a threat?
options: (A) It introduces a virus that destroys the physical hardware of classical computers.
(B) It enables quantum computers to factor massive integers exponentially faster, which would break RSA encryption.
(C) It allows hackers to physically steal qubits from government laboratories.
(D) It proves that all digital communications sent before 1994 were already compromised.
questions.explanation: Teks menyatakan bahwa Algoritma Shor pada komputer kuantum yang kuat dapat memfaktorkan bilangan bulat masif secara eksponensial lebih cepat, sehingga 'dapat menghancurkan enkripsi RSA dalam hitungan jam'.

questions.questionText: The word 'existential' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Minor and easily fixed
(B) Pertaining to existence or threatening survival
(C) Purely philosophical
(D) Historically inaccurate
questions.explanation: Frasa 'existential threat' merujuk pada ancaman yang membahayakan kelangsungan hidup atau eksistensi dari sesuatu (dalam hal ini, sistem keamanan digital modern).

questions.questionText: What does 'decoherence' refer to in the context of quantum computing?
options: (A) The mathematical process of factoring prime numbers.
(B) The intentional destruction of classical bits to make room for qubits.
(C) The phenomenon where qubits lose their quantum state and produce errors due to environmental disturbances.
(D) The successful linking of two qubits across a long physical distance.
questions.explanation: Paragraf terakhir mendefinisikan 'decoherence' sebagai keadaan 'di mana gangguan lingkungan kecil... menyebabkan mereka kehilangan keadaan kuantumnya dan menghasilkan kesalahan perhitungan'.

questions.questionText: What can be inferred about the current state of quantum computing from the passage?
options: (A) Large-scale, practical quantum computers capable of breaking RSA are currently in widespread use by the military.
(B) The technology is still heavily restricted by significant engineering challenges, particularly regarding qubit stability.
(C) Scientists have completely abandoned quantum computing due to the unsolvable problem of decoherence.
(D) Classical computers will soon be updated with Shor's Algorithm to combat quantum threats.
questions.explanation: Karena teks menyatakan bahwa membangun komputer kuantum skala besar 'tetap sangat sulit' karena kerapuhan qubit dan kebutuhan akan 'koreksi kesalahan', dapat disimpulkan bahwa teknologi tersebut masih sangat dibatasi oleh tantangan rekayasa (engineering).

questions.questionText: Why does the author use the term 'elegant mathematical trapdoor' to describe RSA?
options: (A) To imply that RSA is outdated and structurally flawed.
(B) To describe how the algorithm is easy to perform in one direction but extremely difficult to reverse.
(C) To show that hackers frequently use secret backdoors to bypass encryption.
(D) To praise Peter Shor's genius in developing a new type of physical computer.
questions.explanation: 'Trapdoor' (pintu jebakan) dalam kriptografi matematika merujuk pada fungsi yang mudah dihitung dalam satu arah (seperti jatuh melalui pintu jebakan) tetapi sangat sulit untuk dibalik (memanjat kembali keluar tanpa kunci rahasia).

passages.title: Myrmecophytism: The Complexities of Ant-Plant Mutualism
passages.content:
In the dense, competitive ecosystems of tropical rainforests, survival often hinges on intricate symbiotic relationships. One of the most fascinating of these evolutionary adaptations is myrmecophytism, a specialized mutualism between certain plant species (myrmecophytes) and colonies of ants. Unlike facultative relationships which are casual and non-essential, true myrmecophytism is obligate; the plant and the ant are so deeply intertwined that neither can thrive, or often even survive, without the other in their natural habitat.

Myrmecophytes have evolved highly specialized anatomical structures to attract and retain their ant partners. Foremost among these are domatia—hollow, modified structures such as swollen thorns, specialized stems, or leaf pouches that provide the ants with secure, weather-proof nesting sites. Furthermore, to sustain the entire colony, the host plants produce copious nutritional rewards. They exude sugar-rich liquids from extrafloral nectaries (glands located outside the flowers) and generate lipid- and protein-rich food bodies, such as the Beltian bodies found on the tips of Acacia leaflets. 

In exchange for this lavish room and board, the resident ants act as a fiercely aggressive standing army. Ants of the genus Pseudomyrmex, for instance, patrol their host Acacia relentlessly. Upon detecting the chemical signature or physical vibration of an intruder, they swarm en masse. They ruthlessly attack herbivorous insects, sting browsing mammals, and even chew away the encroaching tendrils of parasitic vines and neighboring plants that threaten to shade out their host. This aggressive 'weeding' creates a clear zone around the myrmecophyte, granting it a crucial competitive advantage in the struggle for sunlight and nutrients.

However, evolutionary biology reveals that mutualisms are rarely perfectly harmonious; they are often fraught with underlying tensions and the constant threat of exploitation. Some ant species have evolved to be 'cheaters' in this system. These parasitic ants occupy the domatia and consume the plant's nutritional offerings but exhibit 'castration behavior'—they actively destroy the host plant's flowers to force the plant to reallocate its metabolic energy away from reproduction and entirely into producing more vegetative growth and food bodies for the ants. This dynamic highlights that mutualism is not an idyllic partnership, but rather a delicate, reciprocal exploitation maintained by a balance of evolutionary pressures.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: (A) To argue that tropical rainforests are being destroyed by invasive ant species.
(B) To describe the anatomical structure of the Acacia plant in detail.
(C) To explain the obligate mutualistic relationship between certain plants and ants, including its darker, exploitative aspects.
(D) To propose a new classification system for extrafloral nectaries.
questions.explanation: Tujuan utama teks ini adalah menjelaskan hubungan mutualisme obligat antara semut dan tanaman (myrmecophytism), bagaimana mereka saling menguntungkan, dan juga ancaman dari semut 'penipu' (cheaters).

questions.questionText: How does 'obligate' mutualism differ from 'facultative' relationships, according to the passage?
options: (A) Obligate mutualism is casual and temporary, while facultative is permanent.
(B) Obligate mutualism is essential for the survival of both species in their natural habitat.
(C) Obligate mutualism only occurs in arid deserts, not tropical rainforests.
(D) Obligate mutualism involves three or more species, while facultative involves only two.
questions.explanation: Paragraf pertama menyatakan bahwa tidak seperti hubungan fakultatif yang kasual/tidak esensial, mirmekofitisme sejati bersifat obligat, artinya 'keduanya begitu terkait erat sehingga tak satu pun dapat berkembang, atau bahkan bertahan hidup, tanpa yang lain'.

questions.questionText: The word 'domatia' in paragraph 2 refers to:
options: (A) The aggressive behavioral traits of the Pseudomyrmex ants.
(B) The lipid-rich food bodies produced by the plant.
(C) The hollow, modified plant structures that provide nesting sites for ants.
(D) The chemical signatures emitted by parasitic vines.
questions.explanation: Paragraf kedua secara eksplisit mendefinisikan 'domatia' sebagai 'struktur berongga yang dimodifikasi... yang memberi semut tempat bersarang yang aman dan tahan cuaca'.

questions.questionText: What is the function of Beltian bodies?
options: (A) To trap and digest small herbivorous insects.
(B) To provide a protein and lipid-rich food source for the resident ants.
(C) To secrete toxic chemicals that kill competing plants.
(D) To store water for the plant during periods of drought.
questions.explanation: Teks menyebutkan bahwa tanaman menghasilkan badan makanan kaya lipid dan protein ('lipid- and protein-rich food bodies'), 'seperti badan Beltian yang ditemukan di ujung daun Acacia'.

questions.questionText: The word 'encroaching' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
options: (A) Intruding or advancing beyond proper limits
(B) Withering or dying away
(C) Blooming rapidly
(D) Photosynthesizing efficiently
questions.explanation: 'Encroaching' berarti mengganggu, menyerobot, atau merambat melampaui batas yang wajar ('intruding or advancing'). Dalam hal ini, tanaman merambat yang mulai menutupi inang.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, how do the ants protect the host plant from neighboring plants?
options: (A) By blocking the sunlight from reaching the neighboring plants.
(B) By stealing nutrients from the neighboring plants' roots.
(C) By physically chewing away the tendrils of competing plants to clear a zone around the host.
(D) By emitting a highly acidic liquid that dissolves the soil.
questions.explanation: Paragraf ketiga menjelaskan bahwa semut 'bahkan mengunyah sulur-sulur tanaman merambat parasit dan tanaman tetangga yang mengganggu yang mengancam akan menutupi inang mereka'.

questions.questionText: What characterizes the 'cheater' ant species mentioned in paragraph 4?
options: (A) They defend the plant too aggressively, killing the plant's own leaves.
(B) They refuse to eat the Beltian bodies, preferring to hunt insects.
(C) They occupy the domatia and eat the food but do not defend the plant from herbivores.
(D) They consume the plant's offerings and actively destroy its flowers to prevent reproduction.
questions.explanation: Semut 'penipu' (cheaters) dijelaskan sebagai parasit yang menempati domatia, memakan persembahan makanan, dan 'secara aktif menghancurkan bunga tanaman inang untuk memaksa tanaman merealokasi energi metabolismenya'.

questions.questionText: Why do parasitic ants engage in 'castration behavior'?
options: (A) To force the plant to use its energy strictly for vegetative growth and producing more ant food.
(B) To make the plant completely invisible to browsing mammals.
(C) To encourage the plant to produce a wider variety of flower colors.
(D) To make the domatia larger by hollowing out the flower stems.
questions.explanation: Teks menyatakan mereka menghancurkan bunga 'untuk memaksa tanaman merealokasi energi metabolismenya menjauh dari reproduksi dan sepenuhnya untuk menghasilkan lebih banyak pertumbuhan vegetatif dan badan makanan untuk semut'.

questions.questionText: The phrase 'reciprocal exploitation' in the final paragraph suggests that:
options: (A) Mutualisms are based entirely on conscious altruism.
(B) Each species is essentially using the other to maximize its own evolutionary fitness.
(C) The plant is always the victim in the relationship.
(D) The relationship will inevitably result in the extinction of both species.
questions.explanation: 'Reciprocal exploitation' (eksploitasi timbal balik) menyiratkan bahwa ini bukan persahabatan yang indah, melainkan kedua belah pihak 'memanfaatkan' satu sama lain semaksimal mungkin demi kelangsungan hidup evolusioner mereka masing-masing.

questions.questionText: It can be inferred from the passage that if a healthy myrmecophyte were completely cleared of its aggressive ant colony, the plant would likely:
options: (A) Grow much faster because it saves energy on producing nectar.
(B) Evolve into a carnivorous plant within a few generations.
(C) Be quickly overwhelmed by herbivores, parasitic vines, and competing vegetation.
(D) Immediately produce larger and more colorful flowers.
